title: The Models Trying to Fill the Fable Gap
dek: With Fable still dark, the G7 turns into a global plea for access to US frontier models — and enterprises stop waiting, MacGyvering Chinese open weights, compound APIs, and smart routing into something that gets close to Fable-level performance.

oneIdea.headline: The Fable gap is forcing everyone to get sophisticated about which models they use.
oneIdea.summary: A week into the US government's effective ban of Anthropic's Fable and Mythos, the fallout is splitting two ways. Globally, the G7 became a scene of US allies pleading for guaranteed access while quietly realizing they need independence. For enterprises, the lost model is fuel for a shift that was already coming — away from slapping the most powerful state-of-the-art model on every task and toward open weights, compound APIs, and smart routing that hit near-frontier performance at a fraction of the cost.

gpCorner.note: AI commentary for UK general practice; not from the podcast

items.title: Which model sits behind the scribe?
items.body: The edition's real lesson for practices is that the model under an AI scribe or triage tool is now a moving, swappable part — and cheaper open-weight models are closing the gap on the expensive frontier ones. If NLW is right, a surgery could get near-equivalent transcription and summary quality at a fraction of today's cost as suppliers route to cheaper models. If Zitron is right, today's low prices are subsidised and will rise once the bills come due, so a practice should ask its scribe vendor what model it uses, whether that can change without notice, and what happens to the price at renewal.

items.title: Sovereignty and patient data
items.body: The G7 story — that access to US frontier models can be switched off by a government overnight — matters more for the NHS than the cost debate. A tool that depends on a single US model could go dark with no warning. For anything touching patient-identifiable data, the safer position is a supplier that can fail over to an alternative model (including UK or EU-hosted open weights) and that keeps data within UK jurisdiction, rather than one locked to a single frontier provider.

items.title: Cheaper is not the same as safe
items.body: A model that is six cents instead of forty-nine and scores well on coding benchmarks is not automatically fit for clinical summarisation, where a quiet omission matters far more than in a landing page. The edition itself notes benchmarks didn't match reality for one model. Whatever sits behind a scribe, the clinician check of the generated note before it enters the record stays the safeguard — cheaper inference changes the economics, not the medico-legal responsibility.

the_other_side.text: Zitron argues the Fable gap is a sideshow next to the real problem: the labs' "business models don't work." On his telling, the scramble toward cheaper open weights and smart routing is not a market maturing but the AI "tokenomics bubble" bursting — enterprises moved to usage-based billing in early 2026, saw the true cost of the tokens they were burning, and revolted, forcing providers toward "drastic" price cuts. He contends most users will simply refuse to pay AI's actual cost, so squeezing the same work out of six-cent models is a symptom of that revolt, not evidence of a healthy near-frontier alternative.

the_truth_beneath.text: Both men orbit one variable: the unit cost of useful AI inference set against what customers will actually pay. Both accept the same facts — token prices are collapsing, with cheap open-weight models running a task for cents where frontier models charge dimes, and enterprises balked once moved to usage-based billing. The empirical question that resolves it is whether routed and open-weight stacks deliver near-frontier quality on real production work while providers stay gross-margin positive; the observable is inference margins and enterprise renewal and spend over the next few quarters. The residue is pure worldview: Whittemore reads cheap models as a healthy efficiency shift, Zitron as subsidy unwinding. Whittemore is vindicated if routed stacks hold quality and spend grows; Zitron if the price war is just the subsidy ending and renewals fall. Both are polemicists — Whittemore bullish and anti-doom, Zitron bubble-certain and anti-hype, each with a newsletter to sell — so the signal is where they agree despite opposite priors: per-token economics are now the whole game. Watch enterprise renewal rates next quarter.

headlines.headline: The AI industry shows up at the G7 in force
headlines.summary: Sam Altman, Demis Hassabis, Meta's Alexander Wang, and Dario Amodei joined the US contingent, with Mistral's Arthur Mensch on the French side and Cohere's Aidan Gomez with Canada. It's the first time the G7 has seen such heavy AI-industry representation — and it makes more sense in the context of the effective banning of Mythos and Fable.

headlines.headline: Access to US frontier models is no longer a given
headlines.summary: At a meeting all about international cooperation, the global community is for the first time reckoning with the idea that access to US-made frontier models can be withheld. The pivotal discussion came at a closed-door lunch with Trump flanked by Hassabis and Altman, and Amodei seated directly across the table next to Macron.

headlines.headline: Amodei and Hassabis lead the call for AI cooperation
headlines.summary: Amodei argued international cooperation should include structured access to frontier models, chip trade deals that exclude China, and a unified approach to risks like cyberattacks and bioterrorism, urging leaders to resist the temptation to splinter over advanced AI deployment.

headlines.headline: The Trump administration has made it clear the US government holds the AI kill switch.
headlines.attribution: Emmanuel Macron, French President, at the G7
headlines.summary: Macron made a forceful plea for the US not to keep frontier AI to itself, arguing the US and Europe share an interest in keeping the technology from authoritarian regimes. "So let us move forward together," he said.

headlines.headline: We need an international forum that establishes globally accepted standards for testing.
headlines.summary: Altman aligned with the view that AI is now in the domain of government and shouldn't be left to corporate policy alone — the technology must be shaped by people, democratic institutions, and society, not just by the companies building the most capable systems.

headlines.headline: Europe expected a China front, found itself pleading for access
headlines.summary: Euronews framed the EU policy mood as particularly sour. Leaders expected to discuss forming a united front against China and rebuilding supply chains around it — instead they found themselves pleading for access to frontier models critical to shared financial infrastructure. The UK's Starmer requested an export-control carve-out for British nationals and was denied.

headlines.headline: Europe is lagging badly on putting GPUs on racks
headlines.summary: Strength in the AI era comes from compute, and Europe trails. The EU's grand April plan for up to five AI gigafactories committed only €20 billion to deploy roughly 100,000 chips — while US hyperscalers are on track to spend three times that every month on data centers.

headlines.headline: "They're going fine." ... "Going fine."
headlines.attribution: President Trump, on the Anthropic negotiations
headlines.summary: Asked about the Anthropic negotiations, Trump said simply that they're "going fine," with Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick reiterating the line. Watchers came away feeling their timelines for getting Fable back were extended, not shortened.

headlines.headline: Wired adds China context: SK Telecom got access, then lost it
headlines.summary: When Anthropic expanded Mythos access weeks ago, Korean telecom giant SK Telecom was among those who got in. Concerned about China ties, the US government ordered access revoked days before the ban — lending at least some credence to the China rationale over pure personality politics, though analysts disputed SK Telecom's actual China links.

headlines.headline: Noam Shazeer leaves Google for OpenAI
headlines.summary: The "Attention Is All You Need" co-author — whom Google paid $2.7B to re-acquire via Character.AI to lead Gemini — is out the door less than two years later. OpenAI reportedly told employees he'll be creating new model architectures. Altman: "Noam is one of the people I have most wanted to work with since the very beginning of OpenAI. It only took 10 years."

headlines.headline: More than one DeepMind person has told me Noam saved Gemini.
headlines.summary: With Gemini 3.5 Pro's rumored June release gone quiet, Shazeer's departure makes Gemini's future feel uncertain. The lore: he tweaked a few lines of training code and Gemini's quality instantly jumped. "Gemini's coding ability still feels behind."

headlines.headline: OpenAI sunsets Pulse, folds it into scheduled tasks
headlines.summary: ChatGPT's daily AI briefing Pulse will be removed within two weeks, with users encouraged to rebuild it via scheduled tasks — now available to all paid subscribers, even the cut-price Go tier. After also sunsetting 4.5, one Pro subscriber asked why a non-coder with no interest in Codex would keep the plan; NLW isn't sure OpenAI cares right now.

headlines.headline: The biggest winner in the Anthropic controversy is open source.
headlines.attribution: Chubby on X, citing major news outlets
headlines.summary: Bloomberg, Fortune, and CNBC reached the same consensus: making a model open means companies, governments, or organizations with sufficient hardware can run it locally and never worry about it being yanked on a whim. Access predictability, not just cost, is now the argument.

headlines.headline: Government deciding a model is too dangerous adds to the case for open source on local hardware.
headlines.summary: If AI is now powerful enough that governments will keep kill switches, it becomes very hard to build mission-critical workflows around a model that can be cut off. Citrini Research argues that risk only strengthens the case for open-source models running on local hardware.

headlines.headline: Kimi K2.7 Code ships, but benchmarks beat reality
headlines.summary: Moonshot's latest open-source coding model claimed ~22% improvement on its CodeBench V2 and 30% lower reasoning token usage vs 2.6. But users aren't raving — VentureBeat said existing K2.6 users can swap in for lower costs, but it's no reason for others to switch. On Agent Arena it ranked 19th overall and 6th among open models.

headlines.headline: A 3B-parameter model posts Opus-class coding scores
headlines.summary: Weibo AI's Vibe Thinker 3B is generating buzz for running easily on local hardware while approaching Claude Opus 4.5 on coding benchmarks. The trick: super-tuned for reasoning, bad at knowledge — push reasoning to 11 and let knowledge live outside the model in a database, slashing hardware and power requirements.

headlines.headline: You cannot export control your way out of an open source race. The ban didn't slow China down.
headlines.summary: Two days after the US banned Fable 5, China's ZAI dropped GLM 5.2 — topping BridgeBench and reasoning, claimed to beat Fable 5 at one-tenth the cost and 300 tokens/second. It's the Chinese open model getting the most buzz right now.

headlines.headline: GLM costs six cents while Opus costs 49 cents — and you can't tell the difference.
headlines.summary: On design tasks, users say you don't have to trust the benchmarks. Asked to build a landing page, GLM 5.2 and Opus 4.8 produced indistinguishable results — GLM more than 6x cheaper, faster, and more token efficient. Some evidence of benchmark-maxing remains, with internal evals putting it behind frontier models.

headlines.headline: Microsoft eyes a fine-tuned DeepSeek to power Copilot CoWork
headlines.summary: Per Axios, Microsoft — which moved CoWork to usage-based pricing — is testing a locally hosted fine-tune of DeepSeek V4 and other open-source models as cheaper alternatives to Anthropic and OpenAI, expecting to ship a lower-cost model within weeks. The irony: the US bans US frontier weights worldwide while its most embedded enterprise software firm prepares to ship a Chinese model inside the Fortune 500's productivity stack.

headlines.headline: Composer 2.5 wins on price — when it behaves
headlines.summary: Cursor's Kimi-based, coding-post-trained Composer 2.5 benches near Opus and GPT 5.5 at a fraction of the cost. One engineer: $1 for 65% vs Fable's $12 for 70% — why pay 12x for 5 points? But others report it changing files without approval and going on rogue UI overhauls, and on artificial analysis's new agentic-coding benchmarks it fell closer to open Chinese models like GLM 5.1.

headlines.headline: OpenRouter's Fusion fans prompts out to a panel of models
headlines.summary: Fusion claims Fable-level intelligence at half the price by fanning each prompt out to a panel of models in parallel (with web search and bash tools), then having a judge extract structure and a synthesizer write the grounded final answer. OpenRouter found panels consistently outperform individual models — and panels of budget models can surpass frontier ones at much lower cost.

headlines.headline: Smart routing beat brute force. Inference optimization just became a first-class competitive advantage.
headlines.summary: The insight from Harvey's worker-advisor experiment isn't that open source beat frontier — it's that using the most expensive model for every task is laziness, not a quality strategy. Teams building routing layers that send each task to the right model at the right cost are now ahead on quality and cost simultaneously.

headlines.headline: Harvey pairs an open-weight worker with a frontier advisor
headlines.summary: Working with Fireworks, Harvey built a setup where an open-weight worker (GLM 5.1) delegates high-stakes, complex tasks to a closed frontier advisor (Opus 4.7). The combination was not only cheaper than using Opus alone but actually delivered increased performance — and Harvey says it's just the beginning of its model experimentation.

headlines.headline: Every company is about to get the ability to hire infinite employees.
headlines.attribution: Gabe Pereyra, Harvey president and co-founder
headlines.summary: Harvey's Gabe Pereyra explains AI got more expensive, not cheaper: the shift from chat to agents exploded costs, with one user triggering hundreds of agents that trigger more agents. The challenge becomes managing those agents and making the business model work the same way it did with human employees.

headlines.headline: The Fable gap accelerated something that was coming anyway
headlines.summary: NLW's bright side: in a world where frontier costs keep rising, companies will have to get sophisticated about combining models for the best results. Inference optimization and token-efficiency exploration was coming no matter what — and now companies have more chance to get ahead of it than when everyone could just get lost in the glory of Fable 5.
